title: Expect 100-continue, Early Responses, Body Rejection, and Draining · Guided Systems Studio
summary: A protocol pairing room predicts byte, parser, state, ownership, and cleanup transitions before assembling the first expectation and early-response coordination state machine for expect 100-continue, early responses, body rejection, and draining. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of expectation and early-response coordination state machine while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging,…
objectives: Parse Expect, support 100-continue deliberately, and return appropriate final failure for unsupported expectations.
Send 100 Continue only after header-level policy accepts receiving content and before a final response commits.
Bound client waiting for 100, handle early final responses, and avoid sending content after rejection.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Expectation Grammar Policy
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop protocol team handles case 4142 in a field-normalizing cache with an independent origin parser. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: diagnostics must omit credentials and attacker-controlled metric labels; then minimize a fuzz failure and add it to the deterministic regression corpus. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Expectation Grammar Policy.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for parse expect, support 100-continue deliberately, and return appropriate final failure for unsupported expectations. For Commit to the mission model · Workshop Expectation Grammar Policy, keep Expectation Grammar Policy connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Expect Deadlock Proof
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Expect Deadlock Proof: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop protocol team handles case 4151 in a signed webhook receiver behind an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 gateway. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then lower one byte or time budget and prove rejection occurs before allocation or dispatch. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Test client and server state transitions under delayed, missing, duplicate, fragmented, and rejected expectations without deadlock.

For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Expect Deadlock Proof, keep Expect Deadlock Proof connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Test client and server state transitions under delayed, missing, duplicate, fragmented, and rejected expectations without deadlock.
